title: Hortobágyi palacsinta
subtitle: Hungarian savoury crêpes filled with paprika-braised meat and smothered in cream sauce
excerpt: Thin crêpes filled with spiced minced veal or pork in paprika sauce, then rolled and baked under a coating of paprika cream. One of Hungary's most celebrated dishes, at home both on restaurant menus and at the family table.
sourceNotes: Based on the Hortobágy palacsinta tradition as documented in George Lang, 'The Cuisine of Hungary' (1971) and Gundel's Hungarian Cookbook (various editions). The dish is named after the Hortobágy puszta (steppe) in eastern Hungary. Traditional versions use veal or a mix of veal and pork.

content.text: Hortobágyi palacsinta is two things at once: a crêpe dish and a braised meat dish, assembled and baked together into something more satisfying than either on its own. The crêpes are thin and plain. The filling is a dry-ish paprika mince, veal or pork, cooked until the meat absorbs the spice and the liquid has mostly gone. The rolled crêpes are laid in a baking dish and buried under a paprika-spiked cream sauce, then baked until the cream bubbles and the crêpe edges colour.
It is a dish that rewards planning: crêpes made the day before, filling cooked ahead, assembled and baked when needed. The assembly takes about ten minutes once the components are ready.

Whisk {{plain-flour}} of flour with {{eggs}} eggs, {{whole-milk}} of milk, {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter, and a pinch of salt into a smooth batter. Rest for 20 minutes. Cook thin crêpes in a lightly buttered crêpe pan, about 8 per batch, roughly 22 cm across. Stack on a plate.

Soften {{onion}} finely chopped onion in a little oil for 8 minutes. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, then {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika; stir for 30 seconds. Add {{pork-mince}} of pork mince and break it up with a spoon. Cook until no pink remains. Add {{stock-chicken}} of stock and simmer uncovered for 10 to 12 minutes until the liquid has almost gone and the mixture is moist but not wet. Season with salt.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling along the lower third of each crêpe. Roll up tightly. Arrange seam-side down in a single layer in a buttered baking dish.

Stir the remaining {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika into {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche with a pinch of salt. Pour evenly over the crêpes, making sure the ends are covered. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until the cream is bubbling and the edges of the crêpes are turning golden at the tips.

items.fix: Thin the batter with a little extra milk next time. The crêpes should be flexible and slightly translucent when held to the light.
items.cause: Batter too thick, or crêpes cooked too dry

items.fix: Use full-fat crème fraîche only. The sauce will look a little separated but the flavour is fine; stir once when dishing up.
items.cause: Oven temperature too high or crème fraîche too low in fat
items.symptom: Cream sauce has split in the oven

items.fix: Continue cooking the filling until the mixture comes away cleanly from the pan without sticking in a wet puddle.
items.cause: Not enough of the stock was cooked off
items.symptom: Filling too wet and escaping from the crêpes

items.fix: Start checking at 18 minutes. Pull the dish when the cream is bubbling and the tips are golden; further cooking makes the crêpes tough.

content.text: The Hortobágy is Hungary's great eastern steppe. It is a UNESCO World Heritage landscape of grassland, cattle, and horsemen. The name attaches to this dish as a nod to hearty plains cooking. The recipe is on restaurant menus all over Hungary. It is taught in cooking schools as one of the classic dishes of the national kitchen. It sits in the same family as filled palacsinta, Hungary's version of the French crêpe. Those turn up at every meal, sweet and savoury, and have been part of Central European cooking for hundreds of years.
